body: Patcher is the DEFAULT for MCP install — do NOT delegate config-patching to a tool's own installer (no uniform backup/rollback/remove, vendor installer patches ALL hosts + interactive, cant host-scope). Use install --print-config only to FETCH the block; paw owns the write.
No shell=True for community set install steps (RCE vector). Shim = PRINTED manual steps; paw NEVER auto-execs vendor installers. 0 RCE surface.
L3 memory axis is NON-NEGOTIABLE: cross-host portability + mistake-surfacing ONLY (not general agent memory). jcode does intra-host general better — paw is redundant if it drifts off these 2 axes.

Set-size ceiling: <=2-3 active MCP servers per set. Load-all hosts (Codex/Gemini) load every def at startup (~50 tools = 72K token defs, accuracy drops past 2-3 servers). Count MCP only; non-MCP hooks add 0 defs.

paw is NOT a general MCP installer/registry (Smithery/mcpm/MCPDog do that) — no daemon, no Smithery/mcpm runtime dep, offline-capable. Only the 3 unique layers: curated sets, capability router, lesson-memory.
ONE criterion for every paw decision: reduce tokens OR improve context quality = cut WASTE (idle defs, context rot, dup context). Not fewer tokens better but no wasted tokens = budget left for work.

kernel-unify: on Claude Code, paw injects via the live skill-router bridge (paw_block), NOT a 2nd UserPromptSubmit hook. Deploy edits via integration/skill-router.py then copy over — ~/.claude/hooks/* is nah hard-protected (nah trust does NOT clear it). On Codex/Gemini there is no skill-router so paw router IS the single hook.
